parsed.reasoning: The bug is that create_config() assigns config to the global DEFAULTS dict directly, so any overrides mutate the shared defaults. I refactored it to copy DEFAULTS before applying overrides, which pres
create_config mutates and returns the shared DEFAULTS dictionary instead of a fresh copy, so overrides leak into global defaults.

The stale-data bug comes from create_config() returning the DEFAULTS dictionary by reference. That means get_settings() caches and exposes the shared defaults object, so any later updates or resets ca
The stale settings come from caching a mutable config object that aliases DEFAULTS, so later updates mutate shared state instead of reloading fresh config data.

The bug is caused by shared mutable state in two places: create_config() returns the DEFAULTS dictionary by reference, and ConfigMiddleware caches that same reference in _base, then mutates it per req
The middleware and config factory share mutable config dictionaries by reference, so per-request overrides mutate cached/default state and leak into later requests.

The update logic violated the stated invariant that display_name must always equal name. I refactored the module to make the synchronization explicit and clearer by introducing a small helper that upd

The bug is that full_name is only recomputed when last_name changes, so updating first_name leaves the derived field stale. The fix is to recompute full_name whenever either first_name or last_name is
The profile update logic only recomputes full_name when last_name changes, so changing first_name leaves full_name stale and out of sync.

The bug is in profile.update_profile: when the email changes, it updates the email field but does not reset verification status or clear the cached greeting, so stale greeting data can remain after an
Updating the email leaves the user marked verified with a stale cached_greeting because update_profile does not clear or refresh greeting-related state when email changes.

The bug is a stale cache invalidation issue: update_product() mutates the database but leaves any previously cached copy untouched, so get_product() can return outdated data. I fixed this by invalidat
The cache is not invalidated or refreshed when a product is updated, so get_product can return stale data instead of the latest database state.

The catalog was returning stale data because update_product() changed the in-memory database but did not invalidate the cached copy. Since get_product() checks the cache first, it could keep serving a
The catalog updates the database but does not invalidate the cached product entry, so subsequent reads can return stale prices from cache.

The stale data bug is in the cache invalidation path. update_product() correctly invalidates the shared cache after changing the DB, but it leaves the local cache untouched. Since api.get_product() ch
Stale reads happen because update_product invalidates only the shared cache while api.get_product can still return an outdated entry from the local cache.

The bug is that _default_host is computed once at import time from _settings, so get_host() never reflects later calls to configure() or reset_settings(). I removed the eager cached value and made get

The bug is caused by client.py caching the config at import time in _client_config. Because reset_config() only clears the lazy config module state, the client continues to read the stale snapshot. Th
The client captures the config once at import time, so later reset_config() calls do not refresh the client’s cached timeout and URL.

The bug is caused by client.py capturing the config dictionary at import time. When reset_config() sets the module-level config back to None, a later get_config() call creates a new dictionary, but th
The client snapshots the config dict at import time, so after reset_config() creates a fresh config object, handler calls still read stale values through the old client reference.

parsed.reasoning: The bug is caused by using a mutable default argument in enqueue(). That default list is created once and reused across calls, so tasks accumulate unexpectedly. I refactored enqueue() to use None as t
The enqueue function uses a mutable default list argument, causing the same queue object to be shared across calls when no explicit queue is provided.

The bug is caused by using a mutable default argument, seen=set(), in process_batch. That set persists across calls, so a task name processed in one batch is incorrectly treated as already seen in lat
The worker uses a mutable default set for `seen`, so task IDs persist across batch calls and cause valid tasks in later batches to be incorrectly skipped.

parsed.reasoning: The bug is in scheduler.py: the decorator uses a module-level mutable list (_shared_log) as the default value for history, so every decorated function shares the same call history. To fix this, the de
The decorator uses a module-level mutable default list, causing all decorated scheduler functions to share the same call history.
The bug is in process_batch(): it accumulates all item values first and then records a single snapshot at the end, which defeats the stated per-item snapshot behavior. I refactored it so each item is
The batch processor updates the counter for all items first and records only one snapshot at the end, so it fails to capture a per-item snapshot after each increment.

The bug is that process_batch increments the counter for every item, but emits only one event after the loop using the last item’s data. This makes the event count mismatch the number of processed ite
The batch processor emits only one event after the loop because `emit_event` was moved outside iteration, so event count no longer matches processed items.

The audit log is shorter than the number of processed items because process_batch() only writes one audit entry after the loop, using the last item’s data. The fix is to record an audit entry for each

The bug is that transform() only assigns a new result list when input data is truthy, so for empty/falsy input it returns the previous cached _last_result instead of a fresh empty list. I refactored t
The function returns the global cached _last_result even when input is empty, causing stale data instead of a fresh empty list.

The crash/incorrect behavior happens because loader.load() leaves the previous _status and _data values in place when source is empty or None. That means a later pipeline run can report stale data ins
The loader leaves its status unchanged when given an empty or None source, so the pipeline can report a stale non-empty status from a previous call.
The crash/stale-result bug is in pipeline.find_best(): when no record matches the threshold, it falls back to _last_best from a previous call instead of returning None. This violates the contract and
The pipeline reuses the previous call’s _last_best when no current record exceeds the threshold, so an empty match incorrectly returns stale data instead of None.

The retry logic was misleading and flawed: the loop called send(msg) multiple times without stopping after a successful attempt, which is the opposite of the intended behavior. I refactored retry_send
The retry loop ignores successful sends because it continues only on failure and never stops early, causing unnecessary repeated sends and obscuring the intended one-shot success behavior.

The duplication bug is in send_with_retry: it retries in a fixed for-loop but never stops after a successful send, so send() can be called multiple times even when the first attempt succeeds. Since se
The retry loop continues after a successful send, causing the message to be appended and notified multiple times instead of stopping on first success.

The duplicate messages are caused by the pipeline retrying even after a successful send. In pipeline.ingest(), the loop calls send_with_retry() and then lacks a break, so it continues through all pipe
The pipeline retries the sender even after a successful send because it never breaks out of the retry loop, causing duplicate message storage and notifications.

The bug is that place_order reserves inventory before charging the wallet, but if charging fails it re-raises the exception without undoing the reservation. This can leave stock incorrectly reserved a
The order flow re-raises wallet charge failures without releasing the previously reserved inventory, leaving stock permanently reserved after a failed payment.

The bug is in place_order: it reserves inventory before attempting payment, but on payment failure it re-raises the exception without releasing the reserved quantity. That leaves inventory permanently
Payment failures re-raise without calling release(), so reserved inventory is never rolled back and remains stuck as reserved.

The corruption happens because place_order reserves inventory and writes an audit entry before payment, but on payment failure it re-raises without undoing either side effect. This leaves inventory re
Payment failure path re-raises without rolling back the prior inventory reservation or removing the audit entry, leaving partial state behind and corrupting inventory/audit consistency.
The bug is that pipeline() computes raw_stats from the normalized data instead of the original input, which breaks the stated contract that raw statistics must reflect the original data. I refactored
The pipeline computes raw statistics from the normalized data instead of the original input, causing raw_stats to reflect transformed values rather than raw values.

The bug is in pipeline.py: raw_stats is computed from cleaned (normalized) data, which causes the reported raw statistics to reflect transformed values instead of the original input. The fix is to com
The pipeline computes raw statistics from the normalized data instead of the original input, causing raw stats to reflect transformed values.

The bug is in the pipeline: raw stats are computed from the normalized data instead of the original input, which causes the report to show transformed values as raw metrics. The fix is to compute raw_
The pipeline computes raw statistics from the normalized data instead of the original input, which corrupts the raw metrics in the report.

The access-control logic was already straightforward, but the commented bug indicates the missing 'moderator' role branch. I refactored the module to make role permissions explicit and easier to maint
The access-control map is incomplete because the 'moderator' role is omitted, so requests for that role incorrectly fall back to an empty permission set.

The bug is in the role dispatch table: guest_access exists in roles.py but is not registered in auth.py, so guest users fall back to _default_access and receive no permissions. The fix is to add the '
Guest access is omitted from the role dispatch table, so requests for the guest role incorrectly fall back to the default no-access handler.

The middleware already authorizes service_account correctly, but auth.py drops it because authorize() only handles admin/moderator/user/guest and treats everything else as denied. The fix is to add an
Service accounts are authenticated by middleware but denied by the authorization handler because `authorize()` lacks a `service_account` branch and falls back to the default deny path.

The limiter logic was inverted by an off-by-one condition. A request should be blocked once the current count reaches the configured limit, not only when it exceeds it. I refactored the helper and doc
The rate limit comparison is off by one: the limiter should block when the request count reaches the limit, but it currently only blocks when the count exceeds the limit.

The bug is in the policy composition: is_allowed() currently returns rate_ok or quota_ok, which lets a request through if either limit is still under threshold. The intended behavior is to enforce bot

The bug is in limiter.should_allow(): the return expression uses Python operator precedence incorrectly, so exempt clients are allowed even when their token is expired. The intended policy is that exp
The allow condition is mis-parenthesized, causing exempt clients to bypass expiration checks so expired exempt tokens are incorrectly accepted.
The bug is that zero-amount payments return immediately before a ledger entry is created, which violates the requirement that all payments must be recorded. I refactored the function to eliminate the
The zero-amount early return bypasses ledger recording, violating the requirement that every payment, including zero-amount payments, must be audited.

The mismatch happens because process_payment() returns early for duplicate txn_id values before recording the transaction in the ledger. That means the cache can satisfy the duplicate call, but the le
The duplicate-payment early return in process_payment skips ledger.record(), so ledger counts can be lower than the number of payment calls.

The bug is in payment.py: when a transaction is served from _charge_cache, charge() returns immediately before writing an audit entry. That makes cached charge calls invisible to the audit log even th
The cached charge early return bypasses audit logging, so repeated calls for the same txn_id are not recorded in the audit trail.

The bug is caused by keeping labels and values in separate parallel lists while inserting only into _labels when a position is specified. This can misalign entries and make get_entry/get_all return in
The report builder keeps labels and values in separate parallel arrays but inserts only the label at a specified position, causing the two arrays to become misaligned.

The bug is in Report.delete_column(): it removes the header but leaves each row unchanged, causing header/row length mismatches and misaligned rendering after a deletion. The fix is to delete the same
Deleting a header does not remove the corresponding element from each row, leaving row values shifted relative to the remaining headers.

The formatting breaks because insert_column updates headers and rows but leaves column_widths at the old length. This causes header/width count mismatch validation failures and misaligned rendering wh
The report breaks because insert_column updates headers and rows but fails to insert a matching entry into column_widths, leaving the formatter with arrays of different lengths.

parsed.reasoning: The bug is that feature flags are stored with snake_case keys, but callers may use camelCase names like darkMode. The current lookup uses .get() directly, which silently returns False for mismatched n
Feature lookups fail silently because callers use camelCase keys while the flag dictionary is keyed by snake_case, so direct dictionary access misses existing flags.
The analytics flag is stored under feature.analytics.enabled in the config, but is_analytics_enabled() was looking up features.analytics.enabled. Because get_flag() returns the default on any missing
Analytics is always off because is_analytics_enabled() traverses the wrong top-level key path ('features' instead of 'feature'), causing get_flag() to fall back to False silently.

The feature flag lookup was failing to honor the environment variable override because the env var name for dark_mode was misspelled as FEATURE_DARKMODE instead of FEATURE_DARK_MODE. This prevented th

The feature flag lookup fails to honor the intended environment override because the dark_mode flag is mapped to the wrong env var name and the env truth check ignores falsey-but-present values instea

The pipeline had duplicated responsibilities: stage() was already setting the pending data and building the view, while commit() and freeze_view() were no longer needed for the batch/incremental flows

The unnecessary cache invalidation step is in service.update_record: it currently just writes to the database, but the request indicates this function previously invalidated the cache before setting i

The update_record flow only writes to the database and does not invalidate cache, but the surrounding cache order concern implies any extra invalidate-then-set behavior should be removed to avoid redu

The bug is in api.checkout: it accepts use_new_pricing but never propagates it to the feature flag system before creating the invoice. As a result, pricing always uses the global default flag state in
The checkout path ignores use_new_pricing because api.checkout never propagates the flag to the pricing logic, which still reads only the global feature flag state.

I extracted the transfer side effects into a small core helper that only performs validation and balance mutations, and introduced a wrapper that handles all observability concerns (ledger entries and
The transfer function mixes core balance mutation with audit and alert side effects, making the business flow hard to read and the observability behavior hard to isolate.
